sourceTitle: Building a product requirement document in Notion

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of a product requirement document (PRD) according to the guide?

options.text: To provide detailed technical specifications for developers
options.explanation: PRDs focus on user story and context, not deep technical specs.

options.text: To tell the story of how users will use the product and align stakeholders
options.explanation: The guide states the overarching goal is to tell the user story and get alignment without overly technical detail.

options.text: To serve as a marketing brochure for external customers
options.explanation: Marketing materials are separate; a PRD is an internal alignment tool.

options.text: To replace all design documents and wireframes
options.explanation: A PRD links to design docs but does not replace them.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT listed as one of the five typical attributes shared by PRDs?

options.text: Context
options.explanation: Context is one of the five attributes.

options.text: Goals or Requirements
options.explanation: Goals/Requirements are included.

options.text: User Personas
options.explanation: User Personas are useful but not part of the five core attributes mentioned.

options.text: Dependencies
options.explanation: Dependencies are one of the five.

questions.question: Which Notion feature lets you embed external content such as Figma, Sketch, or GitHub directly into a PRD?

options.text: Synced Blocks
options.explanation: Synced blocks keep content consistent, not embed external tools.

options.text: Database Views
options.explanation: Views organize database data, not embed external media.

options.text: Embed Block (using the /embed command)
options.explanation: The guide mentions you can embed Figma, Sketch, GitHub, etc., directly using embed blocks.

options.text: Toggle List
options.explanation: Toggles hide/show content but don’t embed external sources.

questions.question: How do Synced Blocks help maintain consistency across multiple pages in Notion?

options.text: They create a live link that updates the source block everywhere it’s copied
options.explanation: Synced blocks copy and sync content, so changes propagate automatically.

options.text: They lock the block so no one can edit it
options.explanation: Synced blocks are editable; they just stay in sync.

options.text: They automatically generate a PDF version of the block
options.explanation: PDF generation isn’t a function of synced blocks.

options.text: They duplicate the block into a separate database for version control
options.explanation: Synced blocks don’t duplicate; they keep a single source of truth.

questions.question: When adding a requirements database to a PRD, which property should you use to assign a task to a specific team member?

options.text: Date property
options.explanation: Date tracks deadlines, not assignees.

options.text: Person property
options.explanation: The guide specifically mentions using the Person property for assignments.

options.text: Single select property
options.explanation: Single select is used for priority, not people.

options.text: Formula property
options.explanation: Formulas calculate values; they don’t store assignee names.

questions.question: Which database view is best suited for visualizing tasks on a calendar for big‑picture road‑mapping?

options.text: Board View
options.explanation: Board view groups cards by status or priority, not dates.

options.text: Calendar View
options.explanation: The guide lists Calendar View for seeing tasks on a calendar.

options.text: Gallery View
options.explanation: Gallery focuses on images or page previews.

options.text: List View
options.explanation: List view shows a simple list, not a calendar.

questions.question: What is a recommended strategy for providing context in a PRD according to the guide?

options.text: Only include a one‑sentence summary to keep the document short
options.explanation: The guide encourages rich context, not minimal text.

options.text: Use columns, embedded media, and optional Loom walkthroughs to give comprehensive background
options.explanation: Columns, embeds, and Loom videos are cited as ways to deliver full context.

options.text: Link to an external Google Doc for all background information
options.explanation: While linking is possible, the guide emphasizes keeping context inside Notion.

options.text: Rely solely on the title of the PRD to convey context
options.explanation: Title alone is insufficient for context.

questions.question: How can you collect stakeholder feedback directly within a PRD using Notion's comment system?

options.text: Tag stakeholders with @ in a comment so they see the request in place
options.explanation: The guide describes using @ to request input directly where needed.

options.text: Send an email with a PDF of the PRD and ask for replies
options.explanation: Email is outside Notion’s built‑in comment workflow.

options.text: Create a separate spreadsheet for feedback and link it
options.explanation: While possible, it’s not the direct Notion comment method highlighted.

options.text: Add a new page for each stakeholder’s thoughts
options.explanation: Pages are overkill for simple inline feedback.

questions.question: Why might a single‑page PRD that links out to other resources be more effective than a long document containing all details?

options.text: It reduces file size, making the document load faster on mobile devices
options.explanation: Loading speed isn’t the primary reason discussed.

options.text: It keeps the core narrative concise while allowing readers to dive deeper into specific areas as needed
options.explanation: The guide emphasizes a one‑page PRD with links for depth without cluttering the main story.

options.text: It eliminates the need for any collaboration tools, as all information is static
options.explanation: Collaboration is still needed; linking doesn’t remove that need.

options.text: It ensures that only product managers can edit the document, protecting it from changes
options.explanation: Permission control is separate from document length.

questions.question: If you need to track task priority and due dates while ensuring all team members see up‑to‑date information without duplicate data entry, which combination of Notion features should you employ?

options.text: Create separate databases for priority and dates, then manually copy values between them
options.explanation: Manual copying defeats the purpose of a single source of truth.

options.text: Use a single requirements database with a Single‑Select property for priority, a Date property for due dates, and multiple views (Board, Calendar) to surface the data
options.explanation: The guide recommends one database with priority and date properties and leveraging different views for various perspectives.

options.text: Embed an external spreadsheet for priority and a Notion calendar for dates
options.explanation: Embedding external tools adds complexity and breaks sync.

options.text: Rely on Synced Blocks to duplicate priority and date fields across separate pages
options.explanation: Synced blocks sync content, not structured database properties.
